description: Iraq's invasion halted a combined 4.3 million barrels/day of Iraqi and Kuwaiti output. Average monthly oil prices rose from $17/barrel in July 1990 to $36/barrel by October 1990.
Currency collapse and deep demand destruction across Thailand, Indonesia, and South Korea cratered global oil demand expectations, contributing to a price collapse into 1998.

OPEC ministers meeting in Jakarta (Nov 26-Dec 1 1997) raised the official production ceiling from 25.03 to 27.5 million barrels/day, colliding with the Asian Financial Crisis and helping drive prices under $10/barrel by early 1999.
Attacks triggered aviation shutdowns and severe short-term macroeconomic uncertainty, contributing to weaker oil demand amid a broader global slowdown.
Invasion raised fears over Middle Eastern supply disruption; combined with rising Asian demand, it contributed to the start of a multi-year price rally.
Brent/WTI reached a record intraday peak of $147.27/barrel, driven by strong global demand, tight spare capacity, and speculative investment, just before the global financial crisis hit.

event_name: Lehman Brothers Collapse / Global Financial Crisis
description: Lehman Brothers' bankruptcy triggered a global liquidity crisis and recession, crashing oil prices from their $147 peak into the $30s by December 2008.

event_name: OPEC Declines to Cut Production (Thanksgiving Meeting)
description: OPEC, led by Saudi Arabia, chose to maintain its ~30 million bpd output ceiling rather than cut in response to falling prices, prioritizing market share over price support and accelerating the 2014-2016 price collapse.

OPEC and 10 non-OPEC producers (notably Russia) signed the Declaration of Cooperation in Vienna, marking the first coordinated OPEC/non-OPEC production cut deal since 2001 and supporting a price recovery.

Russia refused to join further OPEC production cuts amid collapsing pandemic-driven demand; Saudi Arabia retaliated with price discounts and a production increase, triggering a price war that sent prices from ~$50 to ~$10/barrel by month end.
Global lockdowns following the pandemic declaration destroyed a large share of global transportation fuel demand, compounding the ongoing Saudi-Russia price war.
A severe shortage of storage capacity at the Cushing, Oklahoma delivery hub caused the WTI May futures contract to briefly trade below zero (as low as -$37.63/barrel) for the first time in history.
Invasion triggered sweeping Western sanctions on Russian energy exports and raised structural supply-security concerns, pushing Brent crude above $100-130/barrel.
